name: Command-Query Separation (CQS)

forces: Predictability of read-only calls ↔ convenience of "do and return"
Clarity of side effects ↔ fewer round-trips
x.structure: Every method is either a command (changes state, returns nothing) or a query (returns a value, no observable side effect) — never both.
antiPatterns: Getters that mutate / lazily initialise observably
Methods that both write and return derived results
x.scale: Method level; informs API design and the larger CQRS pattern.

def: A principle that each operation should either change state (command) or return data (query), but not both — so reads are safe to call freely.
problem: Methods that mutate AND return make it impossible to reason about whether calling them is safe / repeatable.
mechanism: Separate commands (void, side-effecting) from queries (pure, return value). Queries can then be reordered, cached, and called without surprise; commands localise change. Scaled up to whole models, it becomes CQRS.
benefits: Reads are side-effect-free and safe to repeat
Foundation for CQRS at system scale
limits: Some idioms inherently combine both (stack.pop, CAS)
failures: Queries with hidden mutation (lazy init that’s observable)
myths: "CQS and CQRS are the same" — CQS is a method-level rule; CQRS splits read/write models
